title: Comprehensions & Generators
subtitle: Python's killer feature — Streams on steroids

sections.java: // Stream pipeline: filter + map + collect List<String> names = users.stream() .filter(u -> u.isActive()) .map(User::getName) .map(String::toUpperCase) .collect(Collectors.toList());
sections.python: # List comprehension: one readable line names = [ u.name.upper() for u in users if u.is_active ] # map -> expression, filter -> if clause

sections.title: Comprehensions Replace Most Stream Pipelines
sections.content: The shape is [expr for item in iterable if condition]. The expression is your .map(), the if clause is your .filter(). For 80% of Stream pipelines this is shorter and faster (no stream setup overhead). Reach for map()/filter() functions or generators only when laziness matters.

sections.java: // Collectors.toMap / groupingBy Map<String, Integer> lengths = words.stream() .collect(Collectors.toMap( w -> w, String::length)); Set<Integer> unique = nums.stream() .collect(Collectors.toSet());
sections.python: # dict & set comprehensions too lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words} unique = {n for n in nums} # Nested: flatten a matrix flat = [x for row in matrix for x in row]

sections.title: Dict and Set Comprehensions
sections.content: Same syntax, different brackets: {k: v for ...} builds a dict, {x for ...} builds a set. Nested loops read left-to-right: [x for row in matrix for x in row] is the outer loop first, then inner — the same order you'd nest for statements.

sections.java: // Lazy Stream — infinite, then limited Stream.iterate(0, n -> n + 1) .filter(n -> n % 2 == 0) .limit(5) .forEach(System.out::println);
sections.python: # Generator function: yield = lazy stream def evens(): n = 0 while True: yield n n += 2 from itertools import islice for n in islice(evens(), 5): print(n)

sections.title: yield: Lazy Infinite Sequences for Free
sections.content: A function with yield is a generator — it produces values one at a time, pausing between them, like a lazy Stream. It uses constant memory regardless of sequence length, so it can model infinite series or stream a 10GB file line-by-line. (x*x for x in nums) with parentheses is a generator expression — lazy, not a list.

sections.title: Generators Are Single-Use
sections.content: Once you iterate a generator to exhaustion, it's spent — looping again yields nothing. Unlike a List you can't re-walk it. If you need the values twice, materialize with list(gen) first. Same caveat applies to map()/filter() objects in Python 3 (they're lazy iterators, not lists).

sections.title: Anatomy of a Comprehension
sections.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 700 160" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:660px;"><rect x="20" y="55" width="660" height="50" rx="10" fill="var(--surface2)" stroke="#2a2e3d"/><text x="40" y="87" fill="#89ddff" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">[</text><text x="60" y="87" fill="#82aaff" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">u.name.upper()</text><text x="230" y="87" fill="#c792ea" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">for</text><text x="275" y="87" fill="#e2e4ea" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">u</text><text x="300" y="87" fill="#c792ea" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">in</text><text x="335" y="87" fill="#e2e4ea" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">users</text><text x="415" y="87" fill="#c792ea" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">if</text><text x="450" y="87" fill="#c3e88d" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">u.is_active</text><text x="600" y="87" fill="#89ddff" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="16">]</text><text x="110" y="130" text-anchor="middle" fill="#82aaff" font-family="Inter" font-size="11">map (transform)</text><text x="330" y="130" text-anchor="middle" fill="#a78bfa" font-family="Inter" font-size="11">source iterable</text><text x="500" y="130" text-anchor="middle" fill="#c3e88d" font-family="Inter" font-size="11">filter (predicate)</text><text x="110" y="40" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8b8fa3" font-family="Inter" font-size="10">expression</text></svg>

tabs.label: The GIL
tabs.content:
Java threads run truly in parallel across cores. CPython has a Global Interpreter Lock: only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time. So threading helps I/O-bound work (waiting on network/disk) but gives no speedup for CPU-bound work.

# CPU-bound across 8 threads  ~= 1 thread (GIL-bound)
# I/O-bound across 8 threads  ~= 8x (threads wait, not compute)

The instinct Java gave you — "more threads = more CPU" — does not hold in CPython.

tabs.label: Pick the tool
tabs.content:
# I/O-bound, async libs    -> asyncio (1 thread, event loop)
# I/O-bound, blocking libs -> threading (ThreadPoolExecutor)
# CPU-bound, real parallel -> multiprocessing

from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:
    results = list(pool.map(heavy_compute, jobs))
# Separate processes each get their own GIL

tabs.label: async / await
tabs.content:
import asyncio

async def fetch(url: str) -> str:
    async with session.get(url) as r:
        return await r.text()

async def main():
    # run many awaits concurrently on ONE thread
    pages = await asyncio.gather(*[fetch(u) for u in urls])

asyncio.run(main())

A coroutine pauses at await just as a generator pauses at yield — same machinery. Closest Java analog: CompletableFuture or Loom virtual threads.

sections.question: What's the key difference between `[x*x for x in nums]` and `(x*x for x in nums)`?
options: Nothing — parentheses and brackets are interchangeable
The brackets build a list eagerly; the parentheses build a lazy generator
The parentheses version is a syntax error
sections.explanation: Square brackets create a list immediately (all values computed and stored). Parentheses create a generator expression — lazy, computing each value on demand, like a Java Stream. The generator uses constant memory and can be infinite, but is single-use.
